course_title: Resilience and Recovery in Tourism and Events

learning_outcomes.description: Critically examine the challenges for the tourism and events industry in a range of developmental, environmental and societal contexts.
Collaboratively evaluate the needs, responsibilities and ethical behaviour of tourism and events stakeholders, at both local and global levels.
Formulate resilient business strategies to pre-empt and recover from emerging issues in the tourism and events industries.
Respond and adapt to crises and disasters to effectively manage tourism and event capabilities and social responsibilities.

assessments.due_date:
Week 2 - Week 12

Completed during seminars in Weeks 2, 4-12.
assessments.task_description:
Throughout the semester, you will complete a series of in-class worksheets linked to weekly content and your case study selected in Week 1. You will respond to a range of questions and short activities, which may include short written answers, diagrams, tables, reflective comments, applied examples, or brief strategic recommendations. Worksheets will be completed individually during class time and submitted at the end of each seminar.

The use of electronic devices will not be permitted when completing worksheets.

You will complete one worksheet during each nominated seminar. Worksheets must be completed individually (handwritten) during class time and submitted before leaving the seminar.

The worksheets may require you to:

·        apply theoretical concepts to industry examples

·        analyse tourism and events industry challenges

·        identify risks, impacts, or stakeholder perspectives

·        propose resilient or sustainable management strategies

·        reflect on class discussions and activities

Worksheet questions will relate to weekly seminar topics, required readings, class discussions, activities, and your selected case study.

To prepare for each worksheet, you should:

·        review the weekly learning materials before class

·        complete any required readings or preparatory activities

·        remain up to date with contemporary tourism and events industry issues

·        bring materials required for seminar participation

·        be familiar with your selected case study

This assessment is designed to protect academic integrity and assess your understanding of course concepts. The use of electronic devices and AI tools are not permitted when completing this assessment. Use of prohibited materials in this task may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct. Responses should represent your own understanding and work completed in class.

This assessment is graded based on the best eight (8) worksheet submissions. Each submission will be marked using the Marking Rubric.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will submit their completed worksheet to the lecturer at the end of each seminar in Weeks 2-12.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: For students who have been approved for a Student Access Plan, please contact your course coordinator at the beginning of the semester to discuss your plan.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
Week 4 - Week 9

Teams will facilitate during the Seminar in Weeks 4-9 as allocated.
assessments.task_description:
Working in teams, you will design and facilitate an interactive class discussion on an assigned tourism or events concept relevant to the course. Your teams will lead your classmates through a critical exploration of the topic, drawing on academic literature, contemporary examples, and discussion activities that encourage participation, analysis, and reflection. All team members are expected to contribute to the facilitation.

This assessment is designed for you to:

  • Communicate and facilitate discussion on complex tourism and events issues.
  • Build critical thinking skills through analysis of contemporary industry challenges and stakeholder perspectives.
  • Connect academic concepts to real-world tourism and events contexts.
  • Collaborate with others - planning, teamwork, and shared responsibility.

Teams will be allocated by the teaching staff (or self-selected if applicable) and are expected to work collaboratively throughout the planning and facilitation.

Each team member must contribute meaningfully to:

  • Researching the chosen topic.
  • Developing facilitation materials and activities.
  • Presenting and facilitating the discussion.
  • Responding to questions and managing class participation.

Teams are encouraged to establish and document (in the Team Charter) clear expectations, communication methods, and timelines early in the semester.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: For students who have been approved for a Student Access Plan, please contact your course coordinator at the beginning of the semester to discuss your plan.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
28/10/2026 2:00 pm

Posters will will be presented during the Seminar in Week 13. An image of the final Poster and a reference list must be uploaded to Blackboard by 2pm 28/10/2026.
assessments.task_description:
You will develop an academic poster based on an allocated tourism or event crisis/disaster case study. Working individually or in pairs, you will analyse the case using concepts and frameworks from the course, evaluate stakeholder responses, and propose strategies that enhance resilience, preparedness, recovery, or adaptation. You will present your poster during a poster session and participate in an oral question-and-answer discussion.

You may complete this assessment individually or in pairs.

If you choose to work in pairs, you are expected to contribute equitably to the research, poster development, and presentation components of the assessment. Both students should be prepared to answer questions during the oral Q&A.

Where concerns arise regarding unequal contribution, you should notify the course coordinator as early as possible. Evidence of individual contributions may be requested. In cases of significant imbalance, individual marks may be adjusted.

Your poster should:

·        Outline the nature and context of the crisis, disaster, or disruption.

·        Apply relevant course concepts, relevant academic literature and theories to the case study.

·        Evaluate stakeholder impacts and responses (including the immediate and longer-term impacts on tourism, hospitality, events, communities, businesses and/or destinations).

·        Justify evidence-based, practical recommendations (strategies) for improving future resilience, preparedness, response, or recovery.

·        Be professionally presented, using visual elements to communicate key messages (include relevant figures, diagrams, images, timelines, tables, or infographics, where appropriate).

Your poster should move beyond description and demonstrate critical analysis of the case study, supported by relevant academic literature and industry evidence. It should communicate key information clearly and concisely and be suitable for an academic conference-style presentation.

You will participate in a short oral question-and-answer session during the poster presentation.

You should be prepared to discuss the:

·        Application of relevant course concepts and frameworks to your case study.

·        Analysis of your case study.

·        Implications for tourism, hospitality, and event stakeholders.

·        Evidence-base supporting your recommendations.

·        Justification of your recommendations.

Questions may require you to think on your feet and explain how your recommendations would change if key factors in your case study changed.

During the Q&A, you are not permitted to use:

·        Notes or cue cards.

·        Translation software or translation applications.

·        Mobile phones, tablets, smart watches, laptops, or other electronic devices.

·        AI tools or AI-generated responses.

You must answer questions independently and demonstrate your own understanding of the material presented. Use of prohibited materials in the Q&A may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct and/or may result in marks being awarded only for demonstrated understanding of course concepts during the discussion.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit a photo of your poster and your reference list to Blackboard by 2pm on Wednesday 28/10/26.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Systems Thinking

Systems Thinking: everything is connected - a change in one part of the system creates impacts elsewhere, often in unexpected ways.


Learning outcomes: L01
